key_insight: A physically informed phase map shows that hybrid lipid–diblock copolymer membrane morphology is governed by chemical immiscibility, hydrophobic thickness mismatch, and areal density, yielding mixed, laterally phase-separated, unzipped, and polymer-rich states.
engineering_relevance: The framework offers a design guide for tuning membrane composition and block lengths to obtain targeted hybrid membranes for drug delivery, synthetic cells, and membrane-protein applications.
method: Extend monolayer theory to predict diblock copolymer bilayer thickness from both hydrophobic and hydrophilic block lengths
Combine immiscibility, mismatch, and geometric constraints into a morphology phase map
Validate predictions with coarse-grained molecular dynamics across lipid/polymer species, block lengths, temperatures, and compositions
evidence_signal: The theory was checked against coarse-grained molecular dynamics over a broad parameter space and shown to unify previously reported experimental and simulation observations.
watch_item: The phase map is physically grounded but may need re-parameterization when transferring to new lipid/polymer chemistries, temperatures, or coarse-graining levels.
paper_title: Phase Behavior of Unilamellar Hybrid Lipid-Diblock Copolymer Membranes
